name: mido-fix
documentation:
# Mido - MIDI Objects for Python

```{image} https://github.com/mido/mido/workflows/Test/badge.svg
:target: https://github.com/mido/mido/actions
```

Mido is a library for working with MIDI messages and ports:

```python
>>> import mido
>>> msg = mido.Message('note_on', note=60)
>>> msg.type
'note_on'
>>> msg.note
60
>>> msg.bytes()
[144, 60, 64]
>>> msg.copy(channel=2)
Message('note_on', channel=2, note=60, velocity=64, time=0)
```

```python
port = mido.open_output('Port Name')
port.send(msg)
```

```python
with mido.open_input() as inport:
    for msg in inport:
        print(msg)
```

```python
mid = mido.MidiFile('song.mid')
for msg in mid.play():
    port.send(msg)
```

Full documentation at <https://mido.readthedocs.io/>

## Main Features

- works in Python 2 and 3.
- convenient message objects.
- supports RtMidi, PortMidi and Pygame. New backends are easy to
  write.
- full support for all 18 messages defined by the MIDI standard.
- standard port API allows all kinds of input and output ports to be
  used interchangeably. New port types can be written by subclassing
  and overriding a few methods.
- includes a reusable MIDI stream parser.
- full support for MIDI files (read, write, create and play) with
  complete access to every message in the file, including all common
  meta messages.
- can read and write SYX files (binary and plain text).
- implements (somewhat experimental) MIDI over TCP/IP with socket
  ports. This allows for example wireless MIDI between two
  computers.
- includes programs for playing MIDI files, listing ports and
  serving and forwarding ports over a network.

## Status

1.2 is the third stable release.

## Requirements

Mido targets Python 3.6 and 2.7.

## Installing

```
pip install mido
```

If you want to use ports:

```
pip install python-rtmidi
```

See `docs/backends/` for other backends.

## Source Code

<https://github.com/mido/mido/>

## License

Mido is released under the terms of the [MIT license](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MIT_License).

## Questions and suggestions

For questions and proposals which may not fit into issues or pull requests, we
recommend to ask and discuss on [Discussions](https://github.com/mido/mido/discussions).

modules.name: extras.hid_joystick
documentation:
Read from /dev/input/js0 and return as dictionaries.

If you have pygame it is easier and more portable to do something
like:

```
import pygame.joystick
from pygame.event import event_name

pygame.init()
pygame.joystick.init()

js = pygame.joystick.Joystick(0)
js.init()

while True:
    for event in pygame.event.get():
        if event.axis == 0:
            print(event)
```

Init:

> 8 = init?

Time stamp |
(ms since boot) |
--+--+--+-- | -- Button number
f0 fb 37 09 00 00 81 00
f0 fb 37 09 00 00 81 01
f0 fb 37 09 00 00 81 02
f0 fb 37 09 00 00 81 03
f0 fb 37 09 00 00 81 04
f0 fb 37 09 00 00 81 05
f0 fb 37 09 00 00 81 06
f0 fb 37 09 00 00 81 07
f0 fb 37 09 00 00 81 08
f0 fb 37 09 00 00 81 09
f0 fb 37 09 00 00 81 0a
f0 fb 37 09 00 00 81 0b
f0 fb 37 09 00 00 82 00
f0 fb 37 09 00 00 82 01
f0 fb 37 09 00 00 82 02
f0 fb 37 09 00 00 82 03
f0 fb 37 09 00 00 82 04
f0 fb 37 09 00 00 82 05

> > --+-- |
> > : 1 = button, 2 =
> >
> > value (little endian unsigned)
>
> button down
> :

98 f0 2f 09 01 00 01 00 1 down
08 fa 2f 09 00 00 01 00 1 up

2c 6a 31 09 01 00 01 01 2 down
04 73 31 09 00 00 01 01 2 up

48 bf 32 09 01 00 01 02 3 down
f8 c4 32 09 00 00 01 02 3 up

Logitech PS2-style gamepad:

> axis 0 == left stick -left / right (left is negative)
> axis 1 == left stick -up / down (up is negative)
> axis 2 == right stick -left / right
> axis 3 == right stick -up / down
> axis 4 == plus stick -left / right (when mode is off), values min/0/max
> axis 5 == plus stick -up / down (when mode is off, values min/0/max

The + stick has two modes. When the mode light is off, it sends axis
4/5. When mode is on, it sends axis 0/1. The values are -32767, 0, and 32767.

Other axis have values from -32767 to 32767 as well.

documentation: TODO: these tests could be improved with better test values such as

functions.name: tests.midifiles.test_midifiles.test_meta_messages_with_length_0
documentation:
sequence_number and midi_port with no data bytes should be accepted.

In rare cases these messages have length 0 and thus no data
bytes. (See issues 42 and 93.) It is unclear why these messages
are missing their data. It could be cased by a bug in the software
that created the files.

So far this has been fixed by adding a test to each of these two
meta message classes. If the problem appears with other message
types it may be worth finding a more general solution.

functions.name: tests.messages.test_messages.test_set_type

modules.name: examples.backends.rtm
documentation:
Experimental backend for rtmidi-python:

http://github.com/superquadratic/rtmidi-python

- Doesn't work with Python 3.3:

  File "rtmidi_python.pyx", line 61, in rtmidi_python.MidiIn.__cinit__
       (rtmidi_  python.cpp:1214)
  TypeError: expected bytes, str found

- Virtual ports don't show up, so other programs can't connect to them.

- There is no way to select API.

Other than that, it works exactly like the included python-rtmidi
backend.

modules.name: examples.sockets.serve_ports
documentation:
Serve one or more output ports.

Every message received on any of the connected sockets will be sent to
every output port.

Example:

> python serve_ports.py :8080 'SH-201' 'SD-20 Part A'

This simply iterates through all incoming messages. More advanced and
flexible servers can be written by calling the `accept()` and
accept(block=False) methods directly. See PortServer.\_\_init\_\_() for
an example.
!/usr/bin/env python

modules.name: mido_fix
documentation:
MIDI Objects for Python

Mido is a library for working with MIDI messages and ports. It's
designed to be as straight forward and Pythonic as possible.

Creating messages:

    Message(type, **parameters) -- create a new message
    MetaMessage(type, **parameters) -- create a new meta message
    UnknownMetaMessage(type_byte, data=None, time=0)

Ports:

    open_input(name=None, virtual=False, callback=None) -- open an input port
    open_output(name=None, virtual=False,               -- open an output port
                autoreset=False)
    open_ioport(name=None, virtual=False,        -- open an I/O port (capable
                callback=None, autoreset=False)     of both input and output)

    get_input_names() -- return a list of names of available input ports
    get_output_names() -- return a list of names of available output ports
    get_ioport_names() -- return a list of names of available I/O ports

MIDI files:

    MidiFile(filename, **kwargs) -- open a MIDI file
    MidiTrack()  -- a MIDI track
    bpm2tempo()  -- convert beats per minute to MIDI file tempo
    tempo2bpm()  -- convert MIDI file tempo to beats per minute
    merge_tracks(tracks)  -- merge tracks into one track

SYX files:

    read_syx_file(filename)  -- read a SYX file
    write_syx_file(filename, messages,
                   plaintext=False)  -- write a SYX file
Parsing MIDI streams:

    parse(bytes) -- parse a single message bytes
                    (any iterable that generates integers in 0..127)
    parse_all(bytes) -- parse all messages bytes
    Parser -- MIDI parser class

Parsing objects serialized with str(message):

    parse_string(string) -- parse a string containing a message
    parse_string_stream(iterable) -- parse strings from an iterable and
                                     generate messages

Sub modules:

    ports -- useful tools for working with ports

For more on MIDI, see:

    http://www.midi.org/


Getting started:

    >>> import mido
    >>> m = mido.Message('note_on', note=60, velocity=64)
    >>> m
    <message note_on channel=0, note=60, velocity=64, time=0>
    >>> m.type
    'note_on'
    >>> m.channel = 6
    >>> m.note = 19
    >>> m.copy(velocity=120)
    <message note_on channel=0, note=60, velocity=64, time=0>
    >>> s = mido.Message('sysex', data=[byte for byte in range(5)])
    >>> s.data
    (0, 1, 2, 3, 4)
    >>> s.hex()
    'F0 00 01 02 03 04 F7'
    >>> len(s)
    7

    >>> default_input = mido.open_input()
    >>> default_input.name
    'MPK mini MIDI 1'
    >>> output = mido.open_output('SD-20 Part A')
    >>>
    >>> for message in default_input:
    ...     output.send(message)

    >>> get_input_names()
    ['MPK mini MIDI 1', 'SH-201']
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: mido_fix.set_backend
documentation:
Set current backend.

name can be a module name like 'mido.backends.rtmidi' or
a Backend object.

If no name is passed, the default backend will be used.

This will replace all the open_*() and get_*_name() functions
in top level mido module. The module will be loaded the first
time one of those functions is called.

functions.name: mido_fix.frozen.is_frozen
documentation: Return True if message is frozen, otherwise False.

functions.name: mido_fix.frozen.freeze_message
documentation:
Freeze message.

Returns a frozen version of the message. Frozen messages are
immutable, hashable and can be used as dictionary keys.

Will return None if called with None. This allows you to do things
like:

```
msg = freeze_message(port.poll())
```
TODO: these two functions are almost the same except inverted. There
should be a way to refactor them to lessen code duplication.

functions.name: mido_fix.frozen.thaw_message
documentation:
Thaw message.

Returns a mutable version of a frozen message.

Will return None if called with None.

documentation:
Connect to a socket port server.

The return value is a SocketPort object connected to another
SocketPort object at the server end. Messages can be sent either way.

functions.name: mido_fix.sockets.parse_address
documentation:
Parse and address on the format host:port.

Returns a tuple (host, port). Raises ValueError if format is
invalid or port is not an integer or out of range.

methods.name: mido_fix.sockets.PortServer.accept
documentation:
Accept a connection from a client.

Will block until there is a new connection, and then return a
SocketPort object.

If block=False, None will be returned if there is no
new connection waiting.

Mido amidi backend

Very experimental backend using amidi to access the ALSA rawmidi
interface.

TODO:

* use parser instead of from_hex()?
* default port name
* do sysex messages work?
* starting amidi for every message sent is costly

modules.name: mido_fix.backends.rtmidi_python
documentation:
Backend for rtmidi-python:

https://pypi.python.org/pypi/rtmidi-python

To use this backend copy (or link) it to somewhere in your Python path
and call:

    mido.set_backend('mido.backends.rtmidi_python')

or set shell variable $MIDO_BACKEND to mido.backends.rtmidi_python

TODO:

* add support for APIs.

* active_sensing is still filtered. (The same is true for
  mido.backends.rtmidi.)There may be a way to remove this filtering.

modules.name: mido_fix.backends.rtmidi_utils
documentation:
Utility functions for RtMidi backend.

These are in a separate file so they can be tested without
the `python-rtmidi` package.

functions.name: mido_fix.backends.rtmidi_utils.expand_alsa_port_name
documentation:
Expand ALSA port name.

RtMidi/ALSA includes client name and client:port number in
the port name, for example:

    TiMidity:TiMidity port 0 128:0

This allows you to specify only port name or client:port name when
opening a port. It will compare the name to each name in
port_names (typically returned from get_*_names()) and try these
three variants in turn:

    TiMidity:TiMidity port 0 128:0
    TiMidity:TiMidity port 0
    TiMidity port 0

It returns the first match. If no match is found it returns the
passed name so the caller can deal with it.

modules.name: mido_fix.backends.portmidi_init
documentation:
Low-level wrapper for PortMidi library

Copied straight from Grant Yoshida's portmidizero, with slight
modifications.

functions.name: mido_fix.backends.portmidi_init.get_host_error_message
documentation: Return host error message.

classes.name: mido_fix.backends.backend.Backend

methods.name: mido_fix.backends.backend.Backend.module
documentation:
A reference module implementing the backend.

This will always be a valid reference to a module. Accessing
this property will load the module. Use .loaded to check if
the module is loaded.

methods.name: mido_fix.backends.backend.Backend.loaded
documentation: Return True if the module is loaded.

methods.name: mido_fix.backends.backend.Backend.load
documentation:
Load the module.

Does nothing if the module is already loaded.

This function will be called if you access the 'module'
property.

methods.name: mido_fix.backends.backend.Backend.open_input
documentation:
Open an input port.

If the environment variable MIDO_DEFAULT_INPUT is set,
if will override the default port.

virtual=False
  Passing True opens a new port that other applications can
  connect to. Raises IOError if not supported by the backend.

callback=None
  A callback function to be called when a new message arrives.
  The function should take one argument (the message).
  Raises IOError if not supported by the backend.

methods.name: mido_fix.backends.backend.Backend.open_output
documentation:
Open an output port.

If the environment variable MIDO_DEFAULT_OUTPUT is set,
if will override the default port.

virtual=False
  Passing True opens a new port that other applications can
  connect to. Raises IOError if not supported by the backend.

autoreset=False
  Automatically send all_notes_off and reset_all_controllers
  on all channels. This is the same as calling `port.reset()`.

methods.name: mido_fix.backends.backend.Backend.open_ioport
documentation:
Open a port for input and output.

If the environment variable MIDO_DEFAULT_IOPORT is set,
if will override the default port.

virtual=False
  Passing True opens a new port that other applications can
  connect to. Raises IOError if not supported by the backend.

callback=None
  A callback function to be called when a new message arrives.
  The function should take one argument (the message).
  Raises IOError if not supported by the backend.

autoreset=False
  Automatically send all_notes_off and reset_all_controllers
  on all channels. This is the same as calling `port.reset()`.

methods.name: mido_fix.backends.backend.Backend.get_input_names
documentation: Return a list of all input port names.

methods.name: mido_fix.backends.backend.Backend.get_output_names
documentation: Return a list of all output port names.

methods.name: mido_fix.backends.backend.Backend.get_ioport_names
documentation: Return a list of all I/O port names.

Wrapper for backend module.

A backend module implements classes for input and output ports for
a specific MIDI library. The Backend object wraps around the
object and provides convenient 'open_*()' and 'get_*_names()'
functions.

classes.name: mido_fix.backends._parser_queue.ParserQueue

methods.name: mido_fix.backends._parser_queue.ParserQueue.get
documentation: TODO: add timeout?

Thread safe message queue with built in MIDI parser.

This should be avaiable to other backend implementations and perhaps
also in the public API, but the API needs a bit of review. (Ideally This
would replace the parser.)

q = ParserQueue()

q.put(msg)
q.put_bytes([0xf8, 0, 0])

msg = q.get()
msg = q.poll()

functions.name: mido_fix.messages.specs.make_msgdict
documentation:
Return a new message.

Returns a dictionary representing a message.

Message values can be overriden.

No type or value checking is done.  The caller is responsible for
calling check_msgdict().
TODO: should this be in decode.py?

functions.name: mido_fix.midifiles.tracks._to_abstime
documentation: Convert messages to absolute time.

functions.name: mido_fix.midifiles.tracks._to_reltime
documentation: Convert messages to relative time.

functions.name: mido_fix.midifiles.tracks.fix_end_of_track
documentation:
Remove all end_of_track messages and add one at the end.

This is used by merge_tracks() and MidiFile.save().

functions.name: mido_fix.midifiles.tracks.merge_tracks
documentation:
Returns a MidiTrack object with all messages from all tracks.

The messages are returned in playback order with delta times
as if they were all in one track.

methods.name: mido_fix.midifiles.tracks.MidiTrack.name
documentation:
Name of the track.

This will return the name from the first track_name meta
message in the track, or '' if there is no such message.

Setting this property will update the name field of the first
track_name message in the track. If no such message is found,
one will be added to the beginning of the track with a delta
time of 0.

functions.name: mido_fix.messages.decode.decode_message
documentation:
Decode message bytes and return messages as a dictionary.

Raises ValueError if the bytes are out of range or the message is
invalid.

This is not a part of the public API.

functions.name: mido_fix.messages.messages.parse_string
documentation:
Parse a string of text and return a message.

The string can span multiple lines, but must contain
one full message.

Raises ValueError if the string could not be parsed.

functions.name: mido_fix.messages.messages.parse_string_stream
documentation:
Parse a stram of messages and yield (message, error_message)

stream can be any iterable that generates text strings, where each
string is a string encoded message.

If a string can be parsed, (message, None) is returned. If it
can't be parsed (None, error_message) is returned. The error
message containes the line number where the error occurred.

functions.name: mido_fix.messages.messages.format_as_string
documentation:
Format a message and return as a string.

This is equivalent to str(message).

To leave out the time attribute, pass include_time=False.

classes.name: mido_fix.messages.messages.BaseMessage

methods.name: mido_fix.messages.messages.BaseMessage.bin
documentation:
Encode message and return as a bytearray.

This can be used to write the message to a file.

methods.name: mido_fix.messages.messages.BaseMessage.hex
documentation:
Encode message and return as a string of hex numbers,

Each number is separated by the string sep.

methods.name: mido_fix.messages.messages.BaseMessage.dict
mido_fix.messages.messages.BaseMessage.from_dict
documentation:
Create a message from a dictionary.

Only "type" is required. The other will be set to default
values.

methods.name: mido_fix.messages.messages.BaseMessage.is_realtime
documentation: True if the message is a system realtime message.

methods.name: mido_fix.messages.messages.BaseMessage.is_cc
documentation:
Return True if the message is of type 'control_change'.

The optional control argument can be used to test for a specific
control number, for example:

if msg.is_cc(7):
    # Message is control change 7 (channel volume).
Abstract base class for messages.

classes.name: mido_fix.messages.messages.SysexData
documentation: Special kind of tuple accepts and converts any sequence in +=.

methods.name: mido_fix.messages.messages.Message.copy
documentation:
Return a copy of the message.

Attributes will be overridden by the passed keyword arguments.
Only message specific attributes can be overridden. The message
type can not be changed.

methods.name: mido_fix.messages.messages.Message.from_bytes
documentation:
Parse a byte encoded message.

Accepts a byte string or any iterable of integers.

This is the reverse of msg.bytes() or msg.bin().

methods.name: mido_fix.messages.messages.Message.from_hex
documentation:
Parse a hex encoded message.

This is the reverse of msg.hex().

methods.name: mido_fix.messages.messages.Message.from_str
documentation:
Parse a string encoded message.

This is the reverse of str(msg).

methods.name: mido_fix.messages.messages.Message.bytes
documentation: Encode message and return as a list of integers.

functions.name: mido_fix.midifiles.units.tick2second
documentation:
Convert absolute time in ticks to seconds.

Returns absolute time in seconds for a chosen MIDI file time
resolution (ticks per beat, also called PPQN or pulses per quarter
note) and tempo (microseconds per beat).

functions.name: mido_fix.midifiles.units.second2tick
documentation:
Convert absolute time in seconds to ticks.

Returns absolute time in ticks for a chosen MIDI file time
resolution (ticks per beat, also called PPQN or pulses per quarter
note) and tempo (microseconds per beat).

functions.name: mido_fix.midifiles.units.bpm2tempo
documentation:
Convert beats per minute to MIDI file tempo.

Returns microseconds per beat as an integer:

```
240 => 250000
120 => 500000
60 => 1000000
```

functions.name: mido_fix.midifiles.units.tempo2bpm
documentation:
Convert MIDI file tempo to BPM.

Returns BPM as an integer or float:

```
250000 => 240
500000 => 120
1000000 => 60
```

functions.name: mido_fix.py2.convert_py2_bytes
documentation:
Convert bytes object to bytearray in Python 2.

Many parts of Mido such as `Parser.feed()` and
`Message.from_bytes()` accept an iterable of integers.

In Python 3 you can pass a byte string:

```
>>> list(b'')
[1, 2, 3]
```

while in Python 2 this happens:

```
>>> list(b'')
['', '', '']
```

This function patches over the difference:

```
>>> list(convert_py2_bytes(b''))
[1, 2, 3]
```

modules.name: mido_fix.syx
documentation: Read and write SYX file format

functions.name: mido_fix.syx.read_syx_file
documentation:
Read sysex messages from SYX file.

Returns a list of sysex messages.

This handles both the text (hexadecimal) and binary
formats. Messages other than sysex will be ignored. Raises
ValueError if file is plain text and byte is not a 2-digit hex
number.

functions.name: mido_fix.syx.write_syx_file
documentation:
Write sysex messages to a SYX file.

Messages other than sysex will be skipped.

By default this will write the binary format. Pass
`plaintext=True` to write the plain text format (hex encoded
ASCII text).

modules.name: mido_fix.parser
documentation:
MIDI Parser

There is no need to use this module directly. All you need is
available in the top level module.

functions.name: mido_fix.parser.parse_all
documentation:
Parse MIDI data and return a list of all messages found.

This is typically used to parse a little bit of data with a few
messages in it. It's best to use a Parser object for larger
amounts of data. Also, tt's often easier to use parse() if you
know there is only one message in the data.

functions.name: mido_fix.parser.parse
documentation:
Parse MIDI data and return the first message found.

Data after the first message is ignored. Use parse_all()
to parse more than one message.

classes.name: mido_fix.parser.Parser

methods.name: mido_fix.parser.Parser.feed
documentation:
Feed MIDI data to the parser.

Accepts any object that produces a sequence of integers in
range 0..255, such as:

    [0, 1, 2]
    (0, 1, 2)
    [for i in range(256)]
    (for i in range(256)]
    bytearray()
    b''  # Will be converted to integers in Python 2.

methods.name: mido_fix.parser.Parser.feed_byte
documentation:
Feed one MIDI byte into the parser.

The byte must be an integer in range 0..255.

methods.name: mido_fix.parser.Parser.get_message
documentation:
Get the first parsed message.

Returns None if there is no message yet. If you don't want to
deal with None, you can use pending() to see how many messages
you can get before you get None, or just iterate over the
parser.

methods.name: mido_fix.parser.Parser.pending
documentation: Return the number of pending messages.

methods.name: mido_fix.parser.Parser.__iter__
documentation: Yield messages that have been parsed so far.

MIDI byte stream parser

Parses a stream of MIDI bytes and produces messages.

Data can be put into the parser in the form of
integers, byte arrays or byte strings.

modules.name: mido_fix.ports
documentation: Useful tools for working with ports

functions.name: mido_fix.ports.sleep
documentation:
Sleep for N seconds.

This is used in ports when polling and waiting for messages. N can
be set with set_sleep_time().
TODO: document this more.

functions.name: mido_fix.ports.set_sleep_time
documentation: Set the number of seconds sleep() will sleep.

functions.name: mido_fix.ports.get_sleep_time
documentation: Get number of seconds sleep() will sleep.

functions.name: mido_fix.ports.reset_messages
documentation: Yield "All Notes Off" and "Reset All Controllers" for all channels

functions.name: mido_fix.ports.panic_messages
documentation:
Yield "All Sounds Off" for all channels.

This will mute all sounding notes regardless of
envelopes. Useful when notes are hanging and nothing else
helps.

functions.name: mido_fix.ports.multi_receive
documentation:
Receive messages from multiple ports.

Generates messages from ever input port. The ports are polled in
random order for fairness, and all messages from each port are
yielded before moving on to the next port.

If yield_ports=True, (port, message) is yielded instead of just
the message.

If block=False only pending messages will be yielded.

functions.name: mido_fix.ports.multi_iter_pending
documentation:
Iterate through all pending messages in ports.

This is the same as calling multi_receive(ports, block=False).
The function is kept around for backwards compatability.

functions.name: mido_fix.ports.multi_send
documentation: Send message on all ports.

classes.name: mido_fix.ports.BasePort

methods.name: mido_fix.ports.BasePort.close
documentation:
Close the port.

If the port is already closed, nothing will happen.  The port
is automatically closed when the object goes out of scope or
is garbage collected.
Abstract base class for Input and Output ports.

classes.name: mido_fix.ports.BaseInput

methods.name: mido_fix.ports.BaseInput.__init__
documentation:
Create an input port.

name is the port name, as returned by input_names(). If
name is not passed, the default input is used instead.

methods.name: mido_fix.ports.BaseInput.iter_pending
documentation: Iterate through pending messages.

methods.name: mido_fix.ports.BaseInput.receive
documentation:
Return the next message.

This will block until a message arrives.

If you pass block=False it will not block and instead return
None if there is no available message.

If the port is closed and there are no pending messages IOError
will be raised. If the port closes while waiting inside receive(),
IOError will be raised. TODO: this seems a bit inconsistent. Should
different errors be raised? What's most useful here?

methods.name: mido_fix.ports.BaseInput.poll
documentation:
Receive the next pending message or None

This is the same as calling `receive(block=False)`.

methods.name: mido_fix.ports.BaseInput.__iter__
documentation: Iterate through messages until the port closes.

Base class for input port.

Subclass and override _receive() to create a new input port type.
(See portmidi.py for an example of how to do this.)

classes.name: mido_fix.ports.BaseOutput

methods.name: mido_fix.ports.BaseOutput.__init__
documentation:
Create an output port

name is the port name, as returned by output_names(). If
name is not passed, the default output is used instead.

methods.name: mido_fix.ports.BaseOutput.send
documentation:
Send a message on the port.

A copy of the message will be sent, so you can safely modify
the original message without any unexpected consequences.

methods.name: mido_fix.ports.BaseOutput.reset
documentation: Send "All Notes Off" and "Reset All Controllers" on all channels

methods.name: mido_fix.ports.BaseOutput.panic
documentation:
Send "All Sounds Off" on all channels.

This will mute all sounding notes regardless of
envelopes. Useful when notes are hanging and nothing else
helps.

Base class for output port.

Subclass and override _send() to create a new port type.  (See
portmidi.py for how to do this.)

methods.name: mido_fix.ports.BaseIOPort.__init__
documentation:
Create an IO port.

name is the port name, as returned by ioport_names().

classes.name: mido_fix.ports.IOPort
documentation:
Input / output port.

This is a convenient wrapper around an input port and an output
port which provides the functionality of both. Every method call
is forwarded to the appropriate port.

modules.name: mido_fix.midifiles.midifiles
documentation:
MIDI file reading and playback.

References:

http://home.roadrunner.com/~jgglatt/
http://home.roadrunner.com/~jgglatt/tech/miditech.htm
http://home.roadrunner.com/~jgglatt/tech/midifile.htm

http://www.sonicspot.com/guide/midifiles.html
http://www.ccarh.org/courses/253/assignment/midifile/
https://code.google.com/p/binasc/wiki/mainpage
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/2984608/midi-delta-time
http://www.recordingblogs.com/sa/tabid/82/EntryId/44/MIDI-Part-XIII-Delta-time-a
http://www.sonicspot.com/guide/midifiles.html

functions.name: mido_fix.midifiles.midifiles.read_chunk_header
documentation: We can't use the chunk module for two reasons:
1. we may have mixed big and little endian chunk sizes. (RIFF is
little endian while MTrk is big endian.)
2. the chunk module assumes that chunks are padded to the nearest
multiple of 2. This is not true of MIDI files.

functions.name: mido_fix.midifiles.midifiles.write_chunk
documentation:
Write an IFF chunk to the file.

`name` must be a bytestring.

methods.name: mido_fix.midifiles.midifiles.MidiFile.add_track
documentation:
Add a new track to the file.

This will create a new MidiTrack object and append it to the
track list.

methods.name: mido_fix.midifiles.midifiles.MidiFile.length
documentation:
Playback time in seconds.

This will be computed by going through every message in every
track and adding up delta times.

methods.name: mido_fix.midifiles.midifiles.MidiFile.play
documentation:
Play back all tracks.

The generator will sleep between each message by
default. Messages are yielded with correct timing. The time
attribute is set to the number of seconds slept since the
previous message.

By default you will only get normal MIDI messages. Pass
meta_messages=True if you also want meta messages.

You will receive copies of the original messages, so you can
safely modify them without ruining the tracks.

methods.name: mido_fix.midifiles.midifiles.MidiFile.save
documentation:
Save to a file.

If file is passed the data will be saved to that file. This is
typically an in-memory file or and already open file like sys.stdout.

If filename is passed the data will be saved to that file.

Raises ValueError if both file and filename are None,
or if a type 0 file has != one track.

methods.name: mido_fix.midifiles.midifiles.MidiFile.print_tracks
documentation:
Prints out all messages in a .midi file.

May take argument meta_only to show only meta messages.

Use:
print_tracks() -> will print all messages
print_tracks(meta_only=True) -> will print only MetaMessages

methods.name: mido_fix.midifiles.midifiles.MidiFile.__enter__
documentation: The context manager has no purpose but is kept around since it was
used in examples in the past.

modules.name: mido_fix.midifiles.meta
documentation:
Meta messages for MIDI files.

TODO:
     - what if an unknown meta message is implemented and someone depends on
       the 'data' attribute?
     - is 'type_byte' a good name?
     - 'values' is not a good name for a dictionary.
     - type and value safety?
     - copy().
     - expose _key_signature_encode/decode?

functions.name: mido_fix.midifiles.meta._reverse_table
documentation: Return value: key for dictionary.

functions.name: mido_fix.midifiles.meta.encode_variable_int
documentation:
Encode variable length integer.

Returns the integer as a list of bytes,
where the last byte is < 128.

This is used for delta times and meta message payload
length.

variables.name: mido_fix.midifiles.meta._STRING_TYPE
documentation: noqa: F821

classes.name: mido_fix.midifiles.meta.KeySignatureError
documentation: Raised when key cannot be converted from key/mode to key letter

methods.name: mido_fix.midifiles.meta.MetaSpec.check
documentation: The default is to do no checks.

methods.name: mido_fix.midifiles.meta.MetaMessage.copy
documentation:
Return a copy of the message

Attributes will be overridden by the passed keyword arguments.
Only message specific attributes can be overridden. The message
type can not be changed.

methods.name: mido_fix.midifiles.meta.MetaMessage._setattr
documentation: FrozenMetaMessage overrides __setattr__() but we still need to
set attributes in __init__().

methods.name: mido_fix.midifiles.meta.MetaMessage._get_value_names
documentation: Used by BaseMessage.__repr__().
